content.text: Skills

are Cowork's mechanism for reusable goal templates. A skill is a named, parametrised goal that a user invokes with a slash command. Instead of authoring the same 200-word goal from scratch each time you run a deal memo or incident brief, you define the template once as a skill and invoke it with just the variable parts.

Skills exist independently of plugins — you can create personal skills without installing any plugin. When skills are bundled inside a plugin, they become available to an entire workspace. Either way, the execution model is the same: a skill invocation substitutes user inputs into the template and hands the resolved goal to the engine.
This topic explains how skills are defined, how the template syntax works, where skills can be created, and how to design skill templates that produce consistent, high-quality output.
What a Skill Is (and Is Not)

A skill is a goal template. When invoked, it becomes a goal — it does not run alongside the goal or inject into the goal. This distinction matters: a skill is not a pre-processing step or a modifier. It is the entire goal, assembled from the template and the user's inputs.
Skill vs goal
Attribute
Skill
Direct goal
How authored
Once, by skill author; reused many times
Each time, by the goal author
How invoked
/skill-name + fill inputs
Type goal text directly
Variable parts
{{placeholders}} filled at invocation
Inline in the goal text
Editing before run
User can edit the pre-filled template
User types it from scratch
Project memory
Applied automatically on execution
Applied automatically on execution
Plugin instructions
Active if any plugin is installed
Active if any plugin is installed
Reusability
One template → many runs
One-shot; no canonical template
Skill vs project memory
Project memory is always-on context. Skills are on-demand invocations. Both are available to the engine when a goal runs, but they serve different purposes:
Project memory: "This project is for Acme Corp. The primary contact is John Smith."
Skill: "/deal-memo {{account}} {{stage}}" — a reusable workflow you invoke for any account
When you invoke a skill in a project with relevant memory, the engine sees both: the skill template (as the goal) and the project memory (as always-on context)
Skill Template Syntax
A skill template is written in plain natural language with optional placeholder syntax for variable inputs. The placeholder syntax is double curly braces:
{{placeholder_name}}
. When a user invokes the skill, Cowork highlights each placeholder in the pre-filled goal text and prompts the user to fill them in.
Basic template

Skill name: /research-brief
Template:

Research the following topic: {{topic}}.
Target audience: {{audience}}.

Find 4–6 authoritative sources (official documentation, peer-reviewed papers,
or reputable industry publications). For each source, read the page using Chrome
and extract the key claim, supporting evidence, and a one-sentence summary.

Write a structured research brief to a new Google Doc in the shared Drive folder,
with sections: Overview, Key Findings (one per source), Synthesis, Open Questions.
Include the source URL under each finding.
This template has two placeholders:
{{topic}}
and
{{audience}}
. When invoked as
/research-brief
, the user fills in both fields before running.
Placeholder naming conventions
Use snake_case for multi-word placeholder names: {{account_name}}, {{report_period}}, not {{accountName}}
Use descriptive names that make the expected input obvious: {{meeting_date}} not {{date}}
Keep the number of placeholders small — more than 4 is usually a sign the skill template should be split
Optional placeholders that have sensible defaults should document the default in the template prose: "If no {{output_format}} is specified, default to a structured Google Doc"
Complex template example with connectors

Skill name: /competitive-brief
Template:

Prepare a competitive analysis brief for {{competitor_name}}.

1. Load their pricing page at {{competitor_url}}/pricing using Chrome.
   Extract: plan names, monthly prices, key features per tier.

2. Search Gmail for any email threads mentioning {{competitor_name}} in the
   last 90 days. Extract: competitive objections raised by prospects, any
   deal losses attributed to {{competitor_name}}.

3. Search the "Competitive Intel" folder in Drive for any existing notes
   on {{competitor_name}}.

Write a brief with sections:
- Pricing (table: tier / price / features)
- Objections (from Gmail)
- Known Weaknesses (from Drive notes)
- Recommended Counters (3–5 bullet points)

Save to a new Google Doc called "{{competitor_name}} Competitive Brief — {{date}}".
Post a one-paragraph summary to #competitive-intel in Slack.
This template references three connectors (Chrome, Gmail, Drive) and Slack, with four placeholders. The
{{date}}
placeholder is typically filled automatically by a project memory entry that defines the current date, or filled manually by the user at invocation.
Creating Skills

Skills can be created in three places in Cowork: your personal Skills library, a project's Skills panel, or as part of a plugin. The creation flow is the same in all three — you write the name, the template, and optionally define the expected placeholders and their descriptions.
Personal skills
Create personal skills in Settings → My Skills. Personal skills appear in your slash-command picker for every goal you write, regardless of workspace or project. They are not visible to teammates.
Open Settings → My Skills → Create Skill
Enter a skill name (this becomes the /name command — no spaces, use hyphens)
Write the template text; use {{placeholder}} for variable inputs
Optionally add a description for each placeholder
Save — the skill appears in your picker immediately
Project skills
Project skills are created by the project owner from the project settings panel. They appear in the slash-command picker for all members of the project, but only when working in that project context.
Project skills are ideal for workflows specific to one engagement, client, or initiative
They are not bundled into a plugin — they live in the project and are removed when the project is archived
Project owners can create, edit, and delete project skills; other project members can use them
Skills within a plugin

Plugin skills are the distribution mechanism for team-wide or public skills. A skill is authored as part of a plugin definition and becomes available to all workspaces where the plugin is installed. Plugin skills cannot be edited by users — they are managed by the plugin author.
Designing Effective Skill Templates

A skill template is a goal, and the same principles that make a good goal make a good skill. The difference is that a skill must work for many different inputs, not just a single specific case — so the template must be robust to variation in what users supply for placeholders.
State the output explicitly

Never leave the output format or destination implicit. A skill is invoked by many users in many contexts — one user's "write it as a brief" is another's "format it as a table". The template must specify exactly where the output goes and in what format.
Specify the output format: structured doc, bullet list, table, JSON, inline output
Specify the output destination: new Drive doc in [folder], Slack message in #[channel], inline in Cowork
If there are multiple output destinations (Drive + Slack), describe both
Use the placeholder for things that vary; hardcode the structure that should always be the same
Include a fallback
Skills often reference connectors. If the connector is unavailable or the source data is missing, the skill should not fail silently. Design templates with explicit fallback instructions:

If no emails matching {{account_name}} are found in Gmail, note
"No email history found" in the deal memo rather than omitting the section.
Test with edge-case inputs
Before publishing a skill to a workspace or plugin, test it with unusual inputs:
An account name with special characters or a very long name
A date range that returns no results
A URL that returns a 404 or redirects to a login page
A folder that exists but is empty
A skill that works for the happy-path input but fails on edge cases will produce inconsistent results for teammates.
Skill length and complexity

A skill template that is 500+ words often indicates the workflow it encodes is too complex to be a single skill. Consider splitting it into two skills that the user invokes in sequence, or using a subagent goal structure that chains work internally.
Ideal skill length: 100–250 words (enough to specify the full workflow, short enough to be readable at invocation)
If a skill requires more than 4 placeholders, consider whether some should be pulled from project memory instead
For highly complex workflows, encode the constant structure in the skill and document the variable parts in a separate project memory entry
Discovering and Managing Skills

The slash-command picker in the Cowork goal input is the primary way to discover available skills. It shows all skills from personal settings, the current project, and any active plugins — sorted alphabetically with a label indicating the source.
Finding skills from active plugins
Plugin skills are labeled with the plugin name in the picker. If a skill you expect is missing, check:
Whether the plugin is active in the current project scope (plugin indicator in toolbar)
Whether there is a skill name collision — a project-scoped skill with the same name shadows a plugin skill
Whether you are in the correct project — project skills only appear within that project
Editing and deprecating skills
Skill type
Who can edit?
How to deprecate
Personal skill
You
Delete from My Skills; gone immediately
Project skill
Project owner
Delete from project settings; gone for all members
Plugin skill
Plugin author
Publish new plugin version without the skill; workspace admin updates plugin
Marketplace plugin skill
Plugin author
Publish deprecation notice + new version; users see change in plugin changelog
Real-World Skill Library Examples

The most effective teams build a skill library of 5–15 skills that cover their most repeated workflows. Here are complete examples across three functions, showing template structure and the reasoning behind design choices.
Legal: contract risk flag

Skill: /legal-risk-flag
Inputs: {{contract_url}} (URL or Drive path to the contract PDF)

Template:
Review the contract at {{contract_url}} for legal risk.
Read the document using Chrome (if a URL) or Drive (if a Drive path).

For each of the following categories, assess presence and risk level (High / Medium / Low / None):
- Liability caps and carve-outs
- Indemnification obligations
- Intellectual property assignment scope
- Data processing and privacy obligations
- Termination for convenience provisions
- Jurisdiction and governing law

Produce a risk summary table: Category | Present? | Risk Level | Clause Reference.
Below the table, write a "Key Concerns" section with no more than 5 bullet points,
ordered by severity.

Save the output to a new Google Doc in the "Contract Reviews" folder on Drive
with filename "Risk Flag — {{contract_url}} — [today's date]".

Do not include legal advice — this is a flagging tool, not a legal opinion.
Engineering: postmortem draft

Skill: /eng-postmortem
Inputs: {{incident_id}}, {{date}}

Template:
Draft a postmortem for incident {{incident_id}} on {{date}}.

1. Read the PagerDuty incident record for {{incident_id}} using the PagerDuty connector.
   Extract: timeline, affected systems, responders, resolution steps.

2. Search the #eng-incidents Slack channel for messages on {{date}} mentioning
   {{incident_id}}. Extract any additional context, quick fixes, or workarounds discussed.

3. Draft a postmortem with the following sections:
   - Incident Summary (1 paragraph)
   - Timeline (chronological, bullet points)
   - Root Cause (5-whys analysis — go at least 3 levels deep)
   - Impact (user and system impact)
   - Resolution Steps
   - Follow-up Actions (owner, due date for each)

Save the draft to a new Google Doc in the "Postmortems / {{date}}" folder.
Post the incident summary paragraph to #eng-postmortems in Slack.
Sales: call prep brief

Skill: /sales-call-prep
Inputs: {{account_name}}, {{meeting_date}}, {{meeting_type}}

Template:
Prepare a call prep brief for a {{meeting_type}} with {{account_name}} on {{meeting_date}}.

1. Search Gmail for all threads with @{{account_name}} from the last 6 months.
   Extract: key decisions, open commitments, objections raised, stakeholders identified.

2. Search Drive for any account notes or deal memos for {{account_name}}.

3. Load the company website and LinkedIn page for {{account_name}} using Chrome.
   Extract: recent news, product announcements, leadership changes in the last 90 days.

Produce a brief with sections:
- Context: where we are in the relationship (summary from emails + Drive)
- Their News: what is happening at {{account_name}} (from Chrome)
- Our Agenda: recommended talking points for a {{meeting_type}} call (3–5 bullets)
- Open Items: commitments or follow-ups from previous interactions
- Risks: what might derail this meeting (based on email history)

Format as a structured Google Doc in the "Meeting Prep" folder.
If a Drive folder for {{account_name}} exists, save it there instead.
Skills and Connectors: Authorization at Invocation

A skill template can reference any connector — Drive, Slack, Gmail, Chrome, or a custom connector from a plugin. The connector must be authorized by the invoking user for the skill to execute successfully. If a required connector is not authorized, Cowork displays a warning in the goal preview before running.
Connector warnings at invocation

When you invoke a skill, Cowork checks whether all connectors referenced in the template are authorized in your account. If any are missing, the goal preview shows a yellow warning: "This skill requires the Gmail connector — authorize in Settings → Connectors."
You can run the skill anyway — it will fail or skip the steps that need the missing connector
Better practice: resolve the authorization warning before running a skill in a production context
Skills in a team plugin should document which connectors they require so new team members know what to authorize
Skill templates that conditionally use connectors
You can write skill templates that gracefully degrade when a connector is not available:

If the Gmail connector is authorized, search Gmail for threads with @{{account_name}}.
Otherwise, skip the email history section and note "Email history unavailable — Gmail
connector not authorized" in the brief.
This makes the skill useful even for users who have not yet authorized every connector, and signals clearly where the output is incomplete.
Skill Metrics: Measuring Adoption

Cowork tracks skill invocations in the workspace analytics panel (available to workspace admins). Metrics include: total invocations per skill, unique users, and average goal run time. These metrics help identify which skills are high-value and which have been abandoned.
High-invocation skills are candidates for promotion to the team plugin (if currently personal or project-scoped)
Zero-invocation skills after 30 days are candidates for removal — they clutter the picker without providing value
Average run time helps identify skills that are too expensive (consistently > 20 minutes) — consider breaking them into smaller, faster skills
User count helps identify skills that have wide vs narrow adoption — narrow adoption may indicate the skill is too specific to one person's workflow
Summary

Skills are reusable goal templates invoked with slash commands. They replace ad-hoc goal authoring for repeated workflows, ensuring consistent output format and connector usage across many runs and many users.
A skill template is written in natural language with {{placeholder}} syntax for variable inputs
Skills can be personal (Settings), project-level (project settings), or bundled in plugins
When invoked, the template substitutes the user's inputs and becomes the goal — project memory and plugin instructions still apply
Good skill templates specify output format and destination explicitly and include fallback instructions for missing data
Plugin skills are the primary mechanism for distributing skills to a workspace or the broader Cowork community
